title: Oatmeal and honey melt-and-pour soap
subtitle: A simple soothing bar with colloidal oatmeal and raw honey, no lye handling required
excerpt: Melt-and-pour soap base removes the need for lye handling and produces a finished bar in under two hours. Colloidal oatmeal provides fine exfoliation and a silky skin feel; honey adds a very light sweetness to the lather and helps the bar retain moisture.
sourceNotes: Melt-and-pour soap making from Bramble Berry beginner guide (brambleberry.com, May 2026). Colloidal oatmeal skin benefits from the US National Eczema Association advisory (nationaleczema.org). Honey in soap formulation from the National Honey Board (honey.com).

recipe.yieldDescription: Approx 450 g soap (~3 to 4 bars, depending on mould size)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale up proportionally. Honey and oatmeal amounts can be kept to 1 tablespoon per 450 g of base at any batch size.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bars can be used immediately after unmoulding, though a 24-hour rest improves the lather.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt the soap base to around 70 degrees Celsius. Add honey and oatmeal below 65 degrees Celsius to prevent discolouration.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years sealed, 6 months once the bar is in use. Honey in melt-and-pour soap can cause slight discolouration and a soft, slightly sticky bar surface over time. Wrap bars in cling film or wax paper to slow this. Discard if the bar develops a sour smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-saponified glycerin soap base that requires only melting, scenting, and pouring. No lye handling is involved. Available in clear or white opaque versions; both work for this recipe.
Very finely milled oats that disperse in water rather than clumping. The fine milling is what distinguishes cosmetic colloidal oatmeal from quick-cook or rolled oats.

content.text: Melt-and-pour soap is the most accessible entry point into soap making. The base has already been through
production; you melt it, add your extras, and pour. This version adds

for gentle exfoliation and honey for a slightly richer lather. White opaque base works better than clear for this recipe: the opacity masks the slightly brown colour of the honey and the speckling of the oatmeal.

items.prepNote: 1 tablespoon; stirred in below 65 degrees Celsius
1 tablespoon runny honey; stirred in below 65 degrees Celsius
Optional; add at the same time as the honey

content.text: Chop or cube the soap base and place in the pouring pitcher. Melt over a simmering-water bath or in a microwave in 30-second bursts, stirring between each. Stop heating once the base reaches 70 degrees Celsius and is fully liquid.

Allow the base to cool slightly to around 65 degrees Celsius. Stir in the colloidal oatmeal, honey, and lavender essential oil if using. Mix gently to avoid creating bubbles. Pour immediately into the prepared mould. Spritz the surface with isopropyl alcohol if air bubbles appear on top; they will pop on contact.
Leave undisturbed at room temperature for a minimum of 2 hours before unmoulding. The bar can be used immediately once set, though a 24-hour rest gives a firmer, drier bar.

Wrap bars individually in cling film or wax paper once set. Unwrapped melt-and-pour soap absorbs moisture from the air and develops a sticky, sweaty surface, which shortens the bar's life and attracts dust. Wrapped bars keep for 2 years in a cool, dry place. Discard any bar that develops a sour or fermented smell.
